name: New York State (Albany)

actions.title: Senate Bill S9144 – Statewide Data Center Permit Moratorium
actions.summary: Senators Krueger and Gonzalez introduced S9144 to impose a minimum three-year moratorium on permits for new data centers using 20 MW or more, pending a generic environmental impact statement and Public Service Commission rate-impact review.

actions.title: Assembly Bill A10141 – Companion Data Center Moratorium Bill
actions.summary: Assemblymember Kelles introduced A10141, the Assembly companion to S9144, halting data center permits for at least three years and directing the Public Service Commission to minimize utility rate impacts on residential and commercial customers.

actions.title: RAISE Act – Responsible AI Safety and Education Act
actions.summary: Governor Hochul signed the RAISE Act, imposing transparency, safety, and reporting requirements on developers of large frontier AI models operating in New York, with an effective date of March 19, 2026.

contextBlurb: New York State has become a national flashpoint in data center regulation, with paired Senate and Assembly bills (S9144/A10141) proposing the strongest statewide moratorium in the country on new data center permits for facilities using 20 MW or more. As of January 2026, New York's grid operator had 48 large-load projects totaling more than 11 gigawatts in its interconnection queue, driving concerns about grid capacity, rising residential electricity rates, and climate commitments. The state simultaneously enacted the RAISE Act to regulate frontier AI models, reflecting Albany's dual focus on AI infrastructure and AI governance.

name: Rockland County

actions.title: Rockland County Planning Department Disapproval of DataBank Phase II
actions.summary: The Rockland County Planning Department unanimously disapproved DataBank's Phase II expansion application in Orangetown, citing disruption of onsite wetlands, high energy usage, lack of parking, fire safety issues, and zoning non-compliance.

actions.title: Orangetown Planning Board SEQRA Lead Agency Designation – DataBank Phase II
actions.summary: The Orangetown Planning Board designated itself lead agency under SEQRA for DataBank's Phase II expansion review after community members argued the project's multi-town and bi-state grid impacts warranted state or county oversight.

actions.title: Orangetown Planning Board Public Hearing on DataBank Expansion
actions.summary: Residents packed an Orangetown Planning Board hearing to oppose DataBank's proposed expansion near Lake Tappan reservoir, raising concerns about grid saturation, ratepayer costs, and the absence of a full Environmental Impact Study.

contextBlurb: Rockland County, and the Town of Orangetown in particular, hosts a dense cluster of at least four large data centers in its Orangeburg light-industrial corridor, including facilities operated by Bloomberg, JP Morgan Chase, 1547 Critical Systems Realty, and DataBank. Community opposition organized under the Bi-State Datacenter Crisis Coalition has focused on proximity to the Lake Tappan drinking-water reservoir, rising Orange & Rockland utility rates, noise from cooling equipment, and the county's determination that data centers are not permitted by right in the LIO zoning district. Multiple expansion applications remain in active SEQRA review as of March 2026, with residents arguing that the scale of cumulative impacts requires a full environmental impact statement.

name: Genesee County

actions.title: Genesee County Economic Development Center Initiates Review of Stream Data Centers Financial Assistance Application
actions.summary: The Genesee County Economic Development Center board unanimously approved initiating the review process for a financial assistance application from Stream Data Centers for a proposed 2.2-million-square-foot, 500 MW data center campus at the STAMP site in the Town of Alabama, despite significant local and tribal opposition.

actions.title: Community and Tonawanda Seneca Nation Opposition to STAMP Data Center Campus
actions.summary: Residents and the nearby Tonawanda Seneca Nation raised formal opposition to Stream Data Centers' proposed 500 MW campus at the STAMP industrial park, prompting calls for a full environmental review and fueling broader state-level support for the S9144 moratorium bill.

contextBlurb: Genesee County became a focal point of New York's data center debate when Stream Data Centers announced plans for a multi-billion-dollar, 500 MW campus spanning 2.2 million square feet at the STAMP (Science, Technology and Advanced Manufacturing Park) in the Town of Alabama. The project drew significant pushback from local residents, environmental advocates, and the nearby Tonawanda Seneca Nation, and the scale of the proposed power draw became a key data point cited by Albany lawmakers in support of the statewide moratorium bills introduced in February 2026. The county's Economic Development Center initiated a formal financial assistance review in early February 2026 while community opposition mounted.

name: New York County (Manhattan / New York City)

actions.title: NYC Local Law 144 – Automated Employment Decision Tool Restrictions
actions.summary: New York City's Local Law 144, effective January 2023, restricts employer use of AI-driven automated employment decision tools and requires bias audits, establishing one of the first municipal AI-use regulations in the country and a model for subsequent county-level AI governance.

actions.title: NYC Participation in Governor Hochul's Data Center Utility Rate Policy Push
actions.summary: Governor Hochul's 2026 State of the State specifically addressed AI data center expansion and its effect on New York City-area utility costs, directing state agencies to consider rate protections for NYC-area ratepayers served by Consolidated Edison as data center load grows.

contextBlurb: New York City was an early national leader in municipal AI governance through Local Law 144's restrictions on automated employment decision tools, and the city's dense concentration of data center demand in the metro area has made it central to the statewide debate over grid capacity and ratepayer protection. Governor Hochul's 2026 State of the State directly cited rising utility costs tied to data center growth as an affordability concern for New York City residents. With nearly half of New York State's 130-plus data centers located in the NYC metropolitan area, city and state officials are increasingly aligned on the need for rate-impact oversight by the Public Service Commission.
